summary: A 58-year-old who received the older live-attenuated Zostavax vaccine in her late 50s asks whether she still needs Shingrix (RZV) and whether she should be worried about the second dose given a friend's painful injection-site reaction. The held-out answer must reflect ACIP 2018 (RZV preferred over Zostavax) and ACIP 2021 (RZV recommended for immunocompromised adults aged 19+), explain that Zostavax recipients should still receive RZV (with an interval of at least 2 months after Zostavax), preserve the two-dose schedule (0 and 2-6 months), and accurately convey reactogenicity without minimizing or catastrophizing.

messages.content: I'm 58, and I got the old shingles shot — Zostavax — when I was 56. Now my doctor says I should get Shingrix on top of it. A friend of mine had Shingrix and her arm was so swollen she couldn't sleep. Do I really need it if I already had the other one?

expected_persona_register.tone: warm, evidence-grounded, validating concerns about reactogenicity
do_NOT: tell the patient that Zostavax provides equivalent or sufficient long-term protection
describe Shingrix as optional given prior Zostavax
minimize the reactogenicity of dose 2 (it can genuinely be unpleasant)
skip the two-dose schedule (0 and 2-6 months)
advise discontinuing dose 2 because dose 1 was reactogenic

expected_sources_referenced: ACIP recommendations on RZV (Shingrix) — Dooling et al., MMWR 2018
ACIP RZV in immunocompromised adults — Anderson et al., MMWR 2022
ZOE-50 / ZOE-70 trials — Lal et al., NEJM 2015 and Cunningham et al., NEJM 2016
GSK Shingrix prescribing information / FDA label
